_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against AAO Preferred Practice Pattern (Lacrimal Disorders), BMJ Best Practice on epiphora, Ophthalmic Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery DCR meta-analyses, and Cochrane review of congenital NLDO management. Anatomy and surgical success rates checked against Tucker et al. external DCR series and endoscopic DCR meta-analyses.

h1Title: Epiphora (Watering Eyes): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Epiphora is overflow tearing — visible tears running down the cheek because the eye produces more fluid than the drainage system can clear, or because that drainage system is blocked. It affects roughly 1 in 5 adults over age 60 in primary care surveys, with a strong female predominance after midlife due to a narrower bony lacrimal canal. Most patients describe a constant film of tears, blurred vision that clears when they blink hard, and skin irritation along the lower lid. Untreated obstructive epiphora carries a real risk of dacryocystitis, an acute infection of the tear sac that can spread to the orbit. Treatment is highly effective at every level: surface lubrication for reflex tearing, simple punctal dilation for early stenosis, and dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR) — a 90 to 95 percent successful operation — for full nasolacrimal duct obstruction.

keyStats.prevalence: Reported in 13 to 25 percent of adults over 60 attending primary care (UK GP surveys)
keyStats.demographics: Female-to-male ratio about 2 to 3 to 1 in acquired NLDO; congenital NLDO affects 5 to 20 percent of newborns
keyStats.avgAge: Bimodal — infants under 12 months and adults over 50 years
keyStats.globalCases: Tens of millions; the most common reason adults consult an oculoplastic surgeon

definition: Epiphora (ICD-10: H04.2, with H04.20 for unspecified epiphora and H04.21–H04.23 for excess lacrimation, insufficient drainage, and altered tear film) is the overflow of tears onto the cheek that results from any imbalance between tear production and tear drainage. The lacrimal system has two arms: secretion (mainly the lacrimal gland in the superolateral orbit, plus accessory glands of Krause and Wolfring) and outflow (the upper and lower puncta, canaliculi, common canaliculus, lacrimal sac, and nasolacrimal duct, which empties under the inferior turbinate). Failure can occur in either arm. Hypersecretory or reflex epiphora is the eye crying back at an irritated ocular surface — paradoxically, dry eye disease is one of the most common causes, because surface dryness triggers a reflex tear cascade. Obstructive epiphora is mechanical, ranging from punctal stenosis at the very entry point to complete nasolacrimal duct obstruction at the bony canal. Functional epiphora describes patients with an anatomically patent system whose lacrimal pump (orbicularis muscle squeeze, lid apposition, sac negative pressure) is failing. The condition is managed primarily by ophthalmologists, with oculoplastic surgeons handling structural disease and pediatric ophthalmologists managing congenital cases.

typesClassification.description: Tear overproduction triggered by ocular surface disease — dry eye, blepharitis, allergic conjunctivitis, foreign body, trichiasis, ectropion, corneal abrasion, or any irritant. Treatment targets the underlying surface problem; drainage is anatomically normal.

Mechanical blockage anywhere from the punctum to the nasal cavity. Subdivided by level: punctal stenosis, canalicular obstruction, common canalicular obstruction, lacrimal sac pathology, and nasolacrimal duct obstruction (NLDO).

Affects 5 to 20 percent of infants, typically due to a persistent membrane at the valve of Hasner. About 90 percent resolve spontaneously by 12 months with massage and warm compresses; persistent cases respond to probing.

Patent anatomy on irrigation but inadequate tear pump action — seen in lower lid laxity, ectropion, prior facial nerve palsy, or post-stroke orbicularis weakness. Often missed without dye disappearance testing.
Painful, red, swollen tear sac infection arising from chronic obstruction. Requires systemic antibiotics, drainage if abscessed, and definitive DCR once inflammation settles.

primarySymptoms: Constant or intermittent overflow of tears down the cheek, often worse in cold wind, after reading, or in air-conditioned rooms.
Intermittent blurred vision that clears with a hard blink as the tear film redistributes — a very common early complaint.
Sticky mucoid discharge at the inner corner of the eye on waking, suggesting stagnant tear sac contents in low-grade dacryocystitis.
A palpable, sometimes tender lump just below the medial canthus when the lacrimal sac is distended (mucocele).
Skin breakdown, eczema, and redness along the lower lid and cheek from chronic tear maceration of the skin.
Reflux of mucopurulent material from the puncta on pressing over the lacrimal sac — the regurgitation-on-pressure-over-the-sac (ROPLAS) test.
Foreign-body sensation, burning, or grittiness pointing to underlying dry eye or blepharitis as the cause of reflex tearing.
Photophobia and tearing in bright light, especially when surface disease or trichiasis irritates the cornea.
Recurrent unilateral conjunctivitis-like episodes — the stagnant tear sac repeatedly seeds the conjunctiva.
In infants, persistent matting of the lashes and a wet eye from the first weeks of life, sometimes with intermittent yellow discharge.
earlyWarningSigns: Tears beading on the lower lid margin in still indoor air, before any overflow onto the cheek
Needing to dab the corner of the eye repeatedly while reading or driving
Mild crusting at the inner corner on waking, even without a cold or conjunctivitis
Mucus reflux from the puncta when gently pressing over the medial canthus
One eye consistently watering more than the other — an asymmetric pattern strongly suggests obstruction over surface disease
emergencySigns: Acute red, warm, painful swelling at the medial canthus extending toward the nose — acute dacryocystitis that can spread to the orbit
Fever, eyelid swelling, restricted eye movements, or proptosis — possible orbital cellulitis, which is sight-threatening
Sudden vision loss with a watery eye after facial trauma — suggests globe injury or canalicular laceration needing urgent repair within 48 to 72 hours
Bloody tears (haemolacria) or persistent unilateral watering in an older adult with weight loss — must exclude lacrimal sac tumour with imaging

causes.description: The single most common cause in adults over 50. Chronic low-grade inflammation narrows the bony nasolacrimal canal; the duct progressively fibroses until tear flow stops. Female predominance reflects a measurably narrower bony canal in women.

Aging, chronic blepharitis, prior conjunctivitis, herpes simplex, or topical medications (especially long-term glaucoma drops and 5-fluorouracil) can scar the punctum or canaliculus shut. A pinhead-sized punctum becomes a slit, then closes entirely.

causes.cause: Reflex hypersecretion from ocular surface disease
causes.description: Dry eye, blepharitis, meibomian gland dysfunction, allergic conjunctivitis, exposed cornea from lagophthalmos, trichiasis, and foreign bodies all irritate corneal nerves and trigger a reflex lacrimal gland surge. The drainage system is anatomically open but overwhelmed.

Lower lid ectropion (lid turning outward), entropion, lid laxity, and floppy eyelid syndrome pull the punctum away from the tear lake or weaken the orbicularis pump. Tears pool and overflow despite a patent drainage system.

A persistent membrane at the valve of Hasner at the nasal end of the duct is present at birth in 5 to 20 percent of infants. Around 90 percent canalize spontaneously by 12 months; the rest progress to need probing or intubation.
Naso-orbito-ethmoid fractures, dog bites, or canalicular lacerations damage the outflow path. Prior endoscopic sinus surgery, septoplasty, or radiotherapy to the face can also scar the duct.

Rare but important. Squamous cell carcinoma, transitional cell carcinoma, and lymphoma of the sac present as a firm mass above the medial canthal tendon, unilateral epiphora, and sometimes bloody tears. Imaging and biopsy are mandatory.

Granulomatosis with polyangiitis, sarcoidosis, IgG4-related disease, and radioiodine therapy for thyroid cancer can all narrow the lacrimal outflow. A bilateral middle-aged onset with systemic features should trigger this work-up.

riskFactors.description: The bony nasolacrimal canal is anatomically narrower in women, and post-menopausal hormonal change further alters mucosal tone — together producing the 2 to 3-fold higher rate of acquired NLDO seen in women.
Chronic involutional changes in the lacrimal sac mucosa, orbicularis tone, and lid position all rise sharply after the fifth decade, driving most adult cases.

riskFactors.factor: Prior chronic conjunctivitis, blepharitis, or trachoma
riskFactors.description: Recurrent inflammation scars the canalicular mucosa. Trachoma remains the leading global cause of cicatricial epiphora in endemic regions.
Years of preservative-laden eye drops (especially benzalkonium chloride) inflame and stenose the puncta. Switching to preservative-free formulations can stabilize or reverse early stenosis.

Both drugs are secreted in tears and cause direct canalicular epithelial damage; up to 30 percent of patients on long-term docetaxel develop epiphora. Punctal plugs or earlier intubation can prevent progression.
Disruption of the bony canal during injury, or scarring during repair, accounts for a meaningful share of unilateral young-adult cases.

riskFactors.factor: Prior endoscopic sinus or nasal surgery
riskFactors.description: Septoplasty, ethmoidectomy, and inferior turbinate surgery can scar the distal duct or its nasal opening, presenting weeks to months later as epiphora.

riskFactors.factor: Radiotherapy to the head and neck or radioactive iodine for thyroid cancer
riskFactors.description: Both can cause delayed canalicular and ductal stenosis. Risk rises with cumulative dose; some thyroid cancer survivors develop epiphora 1 to 3 years after treatment.
Narrow bony canals and persistent membranes are more frequent. Children with Down syndrome have a higher rate of CNLDO that is less likely to resolve spontaneously.

riskFactors.factor: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis, sarcoidosis, IgG4-related disease
riskFactors.description: Systemic autoimmune and granulomatous diseases are an underrecognized cause of bilateral middle-aged epiphora; screening is appropriate when both eyes are affected and systemic features are present.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 60 in UK primary care report watering eyes in 13 to 25 percent of routine reviews
Female-to-male ratio for adult acquired NLDO is approximately 2.5 to 1
Congenital NLDO is present in 5 to 20 percent of full-term newborns, with 90 percent spontaneous resolution by age one
Trachoma-related cicatricial obstruction remains a major cause in parts of sub-Saharan Africa, the Middle East, and rural Australia
Up to 30 percent of patients on long-term docetaxel chemotherapy develop drug-induced canalicular stenosis
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis starts with a structured history — onset, laterality, position-related worsening, discharge, prior surgery or trauma, topical eye drops, chemotherapy, and systemic disease. A focused slit-lamp examination then identifies surface causes (tear film breakup, blepharitis, trichiasis, foreign body, conjunctival scarring) and lid causes (laxity, ectropion, entropion, lagophthalmos). The next step is a sequence of objective drainage tests. The fluorescein dye disappearance test (FDDT) is the simplest and most informative bedside test: a drop of fluorescein is placed in each eye, the patient blinks normally, and the tear lake is inspected after 5 minutes. Asymmetric dye retention strongly suggests outflow disease. Jones I and Jones II tests follow when functional patency is in question — Jones I checks whether dye instilled in the eye reaches the nose unaided, and Jones II checks whether saline pushed through the canaliculus reaches the nose under pressure. Lacrimal syringing with a cannula then localizes the block: a hard stop with reflux from the same punctum indicates canalicular blockage; a soft stop with reflux from the opposite punctum indicates lacrimal sac or duct blockage. Schirmer testing and tear film breakup time are added when reflex hypersecretion is suspected. Imaging is reserved for atypical, recurrent, or post-traumatic cases — dacryocystography outlines the anatomic block, dacryoscintigraphy quantifies physiologic flow, and CT or MRI rules out lacrimal sac tumours and characterizes bony fractures. Nasal endoscopy is mandatory before any DCR to identify septal deviation, polyps, or unsuspected nasal disease that would alter surgical planning.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Bedside screen for outflow obstruction. Asymmetric dye persistence after 5 minutes is roughly 90 percent sensitive for a clinically significant block.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician places one drop of fluorescein-stained anaesthetic in each eye. You blink normally and look at a screen or chat for 5 minutes. The clinician then shines a blue light at the tear lake and grades the residual dye. Painless and takes under 10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Localizes the anatomic level of obstruction — patent, partial block, canalicular hard stop, or lacrimal sac soft stop with reflux. Confirms the diagnosis in over 95 percent of obstructive cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After topical anaesthetic, a fine cannula is gently inserted into the lower punctum and saline is injected. You feel pressure; you may taste salt water if the system is open. The procedure takes 2 to 3 minutes per eye.

diagnosticTests.test: Jones I and Jones II dye tests
diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates anatomic from functional epiphora. Jones I tests passive drainage; a negative result followed by a positive Jones II indicates functional failure with patent anatomy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fluorescein is placed in the eye (Jones I). After 5 minutes, the clinician swabs the nose to look for dye. If absent, saline is then syringed (Jones II) and the nose is checked again. Mild pressure only; no incisions.

diagnosticTests.test: Slit-lamp examination with tear film assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies surface causes of reflex tearing — meibomian gland dysfunction, conjunctivochalasis, dry eye, trichiasis, corneal abrasion, foreign body, or eyelid malposition driving hypersecretion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You rest your chin on the slit-lamp and the ophthalmologist examines each eye with a bright slit beam. Fluorescein and lissamine green dyes may be added. The exam takes 5 to 10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Schirmer test and tear breakup time
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies baseline tear production and tear film stability when reflex hypersecretion from dry eye is suspected — counter-intuitive but a frequent finding.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small filter-paper strip is hooked over the lower lid for 5 minutes (Schirmer). Tear breakup time is observed with fluorescein under the slit lamp. Mild stinging only.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides anatomic detail of the canaliculi, sac, and duct using a radio-opaque contrast injection. Useful in atypical, recurrent, or post-surgical cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Contrast is injected through the punctum, and X-ray or CT images are taken. Takes 15 to 30 minutes. Discomfort is mild; the contrast can briefly taste odd as it reaches the nose.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Physiologic test of tear flow under near-natural conditions — a tracer drop is placed in the eye and gamma-camera images track movement through the system over 20 minutes. Best test for functional epiphora.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A microdrop of technetium tracer is instilled. You sit in front of the gamma camera blinking normally while images are captured. Radiation dose is very low, similar to a chest X-ray.

diagnosticTests.test: Nasal endoscopy and CT of orbits and sinuses
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies nasal septal deviation, polyps, concha bullosa, sinusitis, or lacrimal sac tumour before any planned DCR. CT is mandatory for atypical masses, post-traumatic cases, or suspected granulomatous disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible endoscope is passed through the nostril after a decongestant spray. CT scan takes 5 to 10 minutes. Both are well tolerated.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of epiphora is mapped directly to the cause identified during the work-up, and decisions follow the AAO Preferred Practice Pattern on lacrimal disorders. Reflex hypersecretory epiphora is treated by fixing the surface — preservative-free lubricants, lid hygiene and warm compresses for blepharitis, topical cyclosporine 0.05 or 0.1 percent for inflammatory dry eye, mast-cell stabilizers and antihistamines for allergic conjunctivitis, epilation or cryotherapy for trichiasis, and lid-position surgery for ectropion or entropion. Surface treatment alone resolves epiphora in roughly 60 to 70 percent of these patients within 8 to 12 weeks. Punctal stenosis is managed first with simple in-office punctal dilation followed by short-term silicone monocanalicular intubation, with success rates around 70 to 85 percent. Membranous distal NLDO in infants is initially managed conservatively — lacrimal sac massage, warm compresses, and a low threshold for topical antibiotics during flare-ups — because spontaneous resolution rates approach 90 percent by 12 months. When CNLDO persists past 12 to 13 months, office or operating-room probing succeeds in 80 to 90 percent at first attempt; balloon dacryoplasty or bicanalicular silicone intubation handles most failures. Partial adult NLDO can be addressed with balloon dacryoplasty, with reported success of 65 to 75 percent in selected cases. Complete adult NLDO is the textbook indication for dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR), which creates a new drainage pathway directly from the lacrimal sac into the nasal cavity by removing the bony lacrimal bone. External DCR via a small medial canthal skin incision has 90 to 95 percent long-term anatomic and symptomatic success (Tucker series); endoscopic endonasal DCR achieves comparable 85 to 95 percent success in modern meta-analyses with no facial scar. Proximal canalicular obstruction that cannot be reopened is bypassed with a Lester Jones tube — a small glass conduit running from the medial canthus straight into the nose — successful in 75 to 90 percent but requiring lifelong tube maintenance.

medicalTreatments.name: Preservative-free artificial tears (4 to 8 times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for reflex epiphora from dry eye or mild surface disease. Stabilizes the tear film, suppresses the reflex secretion cascade, and protects the cornea. Preservative-free formulations are essential when frequency exceeds four times daily.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in 50 to 70 percent of hypersecretory cases within 8 to 12 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical cyclosporine 0.05 or 0.1 percent (twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces ocular surface T-cell-mediated inflammation in dry eye disease, allowing baseline tear film stability to return and reflex tearing to settle. Used long-term; benefit accrues over 3 to 6 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant tear film stabilization and reduced epiphora in 50 to 60 percent of patients with inflammatory dry eye in randomized trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Lid hygiene with warm compresses and lid scrubs
medicalTreatments.description: Core treatment for blepharitis-related reflex tearing. A warm compress for 5 to 10 minutes followed by gentle lid margin cleaning twice daily clears inspissated meibomian secretions and reduces inflammatory cytokine load on the cornea.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom improvement in roughly 70 percent of patients with blepharitis-driven epiphora within 6 to 8 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical antibiotic-steroid combinations (short course)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for acute exacerbations of blepharoconjunctivitis or low-grade chronic dacryocystitis pending definitive surgery. Examples include tobramycin-dexamethasone or chloramphenicol with a short steroid taper.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid reduction in mucopurulent discharge and lid inflammation in 80 to 90 percent of cases within 1 to 2 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic antibiotics for acute dacryocystitis
medicalTreatments.description: Oral co-amoxiclav (or clindamycin if penicillin-allergic) covers the typical staphylococcal and streptococcal organisms; severe or orbital extension requires intravenous therapy and ENT input. Definitive DCR is scheduled 4 to 6 weeks after the infection settles.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Acute infection resolves in over 90 percent of cases; recurrence is the rule unless surgical drainage is then performed.

medicalTreatments.name: Punctal dilation with topical lubrication
medicalTreatments.description: Office procedure for early punctal stenosis. A fine punctal dilator gently opens a scarred punctum; lubricants and lid hygiene then prevent re-stenosis. Often combined with monocanalicular silicone intubation for 6 to 12 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic patency in 70 to 85 percent at 12 months, depending on stenosis severity.

surgicalOptions.name: External dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR)
surgicalOptions.description: Gold-standard surgery for complete NLDO. Through a small medial canthal skin incision, the surgeon removes part of the lacrimal bone and anastomoses the lacrimal sac directly to the nasal mucosa. Usually performed under general anaesthesia; silicone stents may be left for 3 to 6 months.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term anatomic and symptomatic success of 90 to 95 percent (Tucker external DCR series and large modern audits).

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic endonasal DCR
surgicalOptions.description: Performed entirely through the nostril with rigid endoscopes; no skin incision, no scar, faster recovery. Often preferred when the patient also has nasal pathology that can be addressed simultaneously.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pooled success of 85 to 95 percent in recent meta-analyses; results approach external DCR in high-volume centres.

surgicalOptions.name: Balloon dacryoplasty
surgicalOptions.description: A high-pressure balloon catheter is passed through the nasolacrimal duct and inflated to dilate strictures. Best suited to incomplete adult NLDO and to congenital cases that failed simple probing.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 65 to 75 percent in adults with partial obstruction; up to 80 percent in carefully selected paediatric cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Bicanalicular and monocanalicular silicone intubation
surgicalOptions.description: Soft silicone tubes are passed through the canaliculi and duct, stenting them open for 3 to 6 months to allow re-epithelialization. Used in canalicular stenosis, after probing, and as an adjunct to DCR.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 70 to 85 percent in adult canalicular stenosis; higher in paediatric NLDO after probing.

surgicalOptions.name: Lacrimal probing under general anaesthesia (paediatric)
surgicalOptions.description: For persistent CNLDO past 12 to 13 months. A fine probe is passed through the punctum, down the canaliculus, and through the membrane at the valve of Hasner. Confirmed by fluorescein dye recovery in the nasopharynx.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 80 to 90 percent at first probing; secondary probing with intubation rescues most failures.

surgicalOptions.name: Conjunctivodacryocystorhinostomy with Lester Jones tube
surgicalOptions.description: For complete canalicular obstruction or failed DCR. A small Pyrex glass tube is placed from the caruncle directly into the nasal cavity, bypassing the diseased canaliculi entirely. Tube position and patency must be checked at routine intervals.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic success in 75 to 90 percent at 1 year; long-term success depends on tube maintenance and migration management.

alternativeTreatments.name: Crigler lacrimal sac massage in infants
alternativeTreatments.description: Firm downward digital massage over the lacrimal sac, 2 to 3 times daily, generates hydrostatic pressure that helps rupture a persistent valve-of-Hasner membrane. Cochrane reviewed evidence supports massage plus observation for the first 12 months of life.

alternativeTreatments.name: Intranasal saline irrigation for adjacent rhinitis
alternativeTreatments.description: Daily saline rinses reduce nasal mucosal swelling around the duct opening and can modestly improve drainage in patients with chronic rhinitis contributing to functional epiphora.

whySeeSpecialist: An ophthalmologist or oculoplastic surgeon should be involved when watering persists beyond 8 to 12 weeks of surface treatment, when there is asymmetric discharge or a palpable medial canthal lump, after any facial trauma or sinus surgery, when watering coexists with vision changes, or when an infant past 12 months still has a wet eye. Oculoplastic referral is essential before any drainage surgery is considered.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an ophthalmologist with subspecialty training in oculoplastic and lacrimal surgery, a high annual DCR volume (a typical busy unit performs 50 to 200 per year), in-clinic syringing and probing capability, access to dacryocystography or scintigraphy, and either external and endoscopic DCR options or close collaboration with ENT. Ask about audited success rates and reoperation rates rather than relying on marketing claims.
hospitalCriteria: On-site oculoplastic surgery service with at least one fellowship-trained surgeon
Operating microscope and endoscopic sinus instrumentation available
ENT input available for combined endoscopic procedures
Paediatric ophthalmology service for infants needing probing under general anaesthesia
keyFacilities: Tertiary eye hospitals with dedicated oculoplastic units
Multispecialty hospitals with combined ophthalmology and ENT theatres
preventionStrategies: Treat blepharitis and dry eye early with lid hygiene and preservative-free lubricants to prevent the chronic surface inflammation that drives canalicular stenosis
Switch long-term glaucoma patients to preservative-free drop formulations whenever possible to limit punctal scarring
Use protective eyewear in high-risk occupations (construction, metalwork, contact sports) to prevent canalicular lacerations from mid-face trauma
Treat allergic rhinitis and chronic sinusitis actively in patients with intermittent watering to keep the nasal end of the duct decongested
In infants with intermittent watering from the first weeks of life, start daily Crigler lacrimal sac massage to maximize the chance of spontaneous resolution
lifestyleModifications: Use a humidifier indoors during winter to reduce evaporative dry eye and reflex tearing
Take screen breaks every 20 minutes (20-20-20 rule) — sustained reading and screen use halve blink rate and worsen surface dryness
Wear wraparound sunglasses outdoors in wind and cold to limit reflex tearing
Avoid rubbing the medial canthus, which can worsen punctal eversion and lid laxity
Maintain consistent lid hygiene if you have blepharitis — daily warm compresses and lid scrubs, not just during flares
After any lacrimal surgery, follow the surgeon's nasal saline and topical antibiotic schedule precisely to protect the new ostium during healing

recommended: Oily fish (salmon, sardines, mackerel) twice weekly — omega-3 fatty acids reduce meibomian gland dysfunction in randomized trials
Flaxseed and walnuts as plant omega-3 sources for vegetarians
Adequate daily water intake of around 2 litres to support tear film volume
Leafy greens and orange vegetables for lutein and vitamin A, supporting corneal surface health
Vitamin D-rich foods or supplementation if deficient — low vitamin D is associated with more severe dry eye in observational studies
avoid: Excess caffeine and alcohol, which mildly worsen evaporative dry eye
Highly processed foods and trans fats that raise systemic inflammatory markers
Tobacco smoke exposure (active or second-hand), which destabilizes the tear film
Long unbroken screen sessions while eating — encourages skipped blinks and surface drying

exerciseGuidelines: There are no exercise restrictions in epiphora. Regular aerobic activity supports overall ocular surface health by reducing systemic inflammation. After DCR or Lester Jones tube placement, avoid heavy nose-blowing and contact sports for 2 to 4 weeks per the surgeon's instructions to protect the new ostium.
dailyManagement: Use preservative-free lubricants on a fixed schedule rather than only when symptomatic
Perform 5 to 10 minute warm compresses morning and evening if blepharitis is present
Massage the lacrimal sac region in infants 2 to 3 times daily until obstruction resolves or probing is scheduled
Keep a tissue or soft cotton pad ready to dab — avoid wiping aggressively, which damages skin
Note any change in discharge colour, swelling, or pain over the sac and contact the eye service promptly if dacryocystitis develops
prognosis: The outlook in epiphora is excellent when the cause is identified and treated. Reflex hypersecretory epiphora responds to surface treatment in 60 to 70 percent of patients within 8 to 12 weeks, with the remainder usually controlled by adding cyclosporine, lid surgery, or punctal procedures. Congenital NLDO carries a 90 percent spontaneous resolution rate by 12 months; for the minority who need probing, success approaches 90 percent at first attempt and over 95 percent cumulatively with intubation or balloon dacryoplasty for failures. Adult complete NLDO is reliably cured by DCR — long-term symptomatic success is 90 to 95 percent for external DCR and 85 to 95 percent for endoscopic endonasal DCR, with comparable quality-of-life gains across approaches. Functional epiphora is more variable and often requires combined lid-tightening surgery and intubation. Untreated obstructive epiphora rarely threatens vision directly but predisposes to recurrent dacryocystitis, mucocele formation, chronic skin maceration, and very rarely orbital cellulitis. Patients with persistent unilateral epiphora over age 50 should always be assessed at least once to exclude lacrimal sac tumour.
recoveryTimeline: After DCR, most patients notice immediate improvement in tearing once swelling settles over 1 to 2 weeks. The final result is judged at 3 to 6 months, after silicone stents (if used) are removed. Skin incision scars from external DCR fade to a fine line within 6 to 12 months. Probing in infants typically results in a dry eye within days. Lester Jones tube patients need a 6-week settling period and lifelong tube checks every 6 to 12 months.
complications: Acute dacryocystitis — a painful, red, tender swelling over the lacrimal sac that can spread to the orbit if not treated promptly with systemic antibiotics and drainage
Chronic mucocele formation with persistent discharge and skin breakdown over the medial canthus
Pre-septal and orbital cellulitis spreading from an infected lacrimal sac, which can threaten vision and requires urgent intravenous antibiotics
Persistent skin eczema and contact dermatitis along the lower lid and cheek from chronic tear maceration
Recurrence after DCR (5 to 15 percent), usually from scar tissue at the new ostium and treated with revision surgery or intubation
Lester Jones tube migration, extrusion, or blockage requiring repositioning or replacement
Missed lacrimal sac tumour in adults with chronic unilateral epiphora — bloody tears, a firm mass, or unexplained recurrence should trigger imaging and biopsy

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — EyeSmart: Watery Eyes
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the body that writes the lacrimal Preferred Practice Pattern.

supportResources.name: Royal College of Ophthalmologists (UK) — Lacrimal Disorders Patient Information
supportResources.description: UK professional body patient education on watering eyes and DCR surgery.

supportResources.name: Moorfields Eye Hospital — Watery Eye Information
supportResources.description: Patient-facing explanation of diagnosis and DCR from a leading specialist eye hospital.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic Rhinitis (Hay Fever)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic rhinitis causes bilateral itchy, watery eyes alongside sneezing, nasal itch, and clear rhinorrhoea, all triggered by allergens and improving with antihistamines. Obstructive epiphora is typically unilateral, unrelated to allergens, often accompanied by mucoid discharge from the puncta on sac pressure, and does not respond to antihistamines.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rosacea (with ocular involvement)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ocular rosacea presents with bilateral burning, gritty, watering eyes, telangiectasias and crusting at the lid margins, and facial erythema with papules and pustules. Reflex tearing here is driven by meibomian gland dysfunction; the drainage system is anatomically open and management is lid hygiene plus oral doxycycline.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sjogren Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sjogren syndrome causes paradoxical reflex tearing on a background of severe aqueous-deficient dry eye, plus a dry mouth, joint symptoms, and positive anti-Ro/anti-La antibodies. The Schirmer test is markedly low (under 5 mm) and tear breakup time is short, distinguishing it from obstructive epiphora, where Schirmer values are normal or high.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bell Palsy (with lagophthalmos)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Facial nerve palsy weakens the orbicularis oculi, producing lagophthalmos and a flaccid lower lid that fails to pump tears. Watering is unilateral and accompanied by an obvious facial droop and incomplete lid closure, which obstructive epiphora does not show.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic nasal inflammation and septal deviation reduce drainage at the nasal opening of the duct and complicate DCR planning.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Rhinitis (Hay Fever)
relatedConditions.relevance: Allergic nasal and conjunctival inflammation is a frequent driver of bilateral reflex tearing and is a common comorbidity that must be controlled before any surgical decision.

relatedConditions.name: Rosacea
relatedConditions.relevance: Ocular rosacea is one of the most common surface causes of chronic reflex epiphora in middle-aged adults.

relatedConditions.name: Sjogren Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Paradoxical reflex tearing in severe aqueous-deficient dry eye is a recognised pattern; treatment is directed at the underlying surface disease, not at the drainage system.

relatedConditions.name: Bell Palsy
relatedConditions.relevance: Facial nerve palsy disables the lacrimal pump and causes functional epiphora that improves with lid-tightening procedures rather than DCR.

faqs.question: What is epiphora in simple terms?
faqs.answer: Epiphora is the medical term for watering eyes — tears overflowing down the cheek because the eye either makes too many tears (often as a reflex against a sore surface) or because the drainage channel from the eye to the nose is partly or fully blocked.

faqs.question: Why is only one of my eyes watering?
faqs.answer: Persistent watering in one eye usually points to a mechanical blockage in the drainage channel on that side, such as a stenosed punctum or blocked nasolacrimal duct. It can also reflect a single irritated cornea or a misaligned lid. Bilateral watering more often reflects allergy or dry eye.

faqs.question: Can dry eyes really cause watery eyes?
faqs.answer: Yes — paradoxically. When the ocular surface is too dry, sensory nerves in the cornea trigger a reflex burst of tears from the lacrimal gland. The drainage system gets overwhelmed even though baseline tear production is low. Treating the dry eye settles the watering.

faqs.question: When should I worry about a baby with a watery eye?
faqs.answer: Congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction affects up to 1 in 5 newborns and resolves on its own in about 90 percent by 12 months. Seek urgent review if the eye becomes red and painful, the medial canthus swells, vision seems affected, or there is fever — possible dacryocystitis.

faqs.question: What is dacryocystitis?
faqs.answer: Dacryocystitis is infection of the tear sac. It presents as a painful, red, tender swelling just below the inner corner of the eye, sometimes with fever. It arises when a chronically blocked duct allows bacteria to stagnate. Antibiotics, drainage, and later DCR are needed.

faqs.question: How is the cause of watery eyes diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines history, slit-lamp examination, a fluorescein dye disappearance test, and lacrimal syringing. Jones tests, dacryocystography, or scintigraphy are added when functional or partial obstruction is suspected. Nasal endoscopy and CT are used in atypical cases.

faqs.question: What is a DCR operation?
faqs.answer: DCR stands for dacryocystorhinostomy — the standard operation for a blocked tear duct. The surgeon creates a new opening from the lacrimal sac into the nasal cavity by removing a small piece of lacrimal bone. It can be done through a skin incision or entirely through the nose with endoscopes.

faqs.question: How successful is DCR surgery?
faqs.answer: Modern series report long-term symptomatic success of 90 to 95 percent for external DCR and 85 to 95 percent for endoscopic endonasal DCR. Results approach equivalence in high-volume centres. Recurrence (5 to 15 percent) is usually due to scarring at the new opening and can be revised.

faqs.question: Does DCR leave a visible scar?
faqs.answer: External DCR leaves a 10 to 12 millimetre scar at the side of the nose that typically fades to a fine line within 6 to 12 months. Endoscopic endonasal DCR is performed entirely through the nostril and leaves no skin scar at all, which is one of its main attractions.

faqs.question: What is balloon dacryoplasty?
faqs.answer: Balloon dacryoplasty is a less invasive procedure for partial blockage. A high-pressure balloon catheter is passed through the duct under anaesthesia and inflated to dilate the narrowing. It is most useful in incomplete adult obstruction, with success around 65 to 80 percent.

faqs.question: What is a Lester Jones tube?
faqs.answer: A Lester Jones tube is a small Pyrex glass conduit placed from the inner corner of the eye directly into the nasal cavity. It is used when the canaliculi themselves are blocked and cannot be reopened. It works in 75 to 90 percent of patients but needs lifelong checks for position and patency.

faqs.question: Are watery eyes a sign of something serious?
faqs.answer: Most watering eyes are not dangerous, but persistent unilateral epiphora in an adult over 50, bloody tears, a firm mass at the inner corner, or unexplained recurrence after surgery must be investigated with imaging to exclude a rare lacrimal sac tumour.

faqs.question: Will my baby need surgery for a blocked tear duct?
faqs.answer: Probably not. About 90 percent of congenital nasolacrimal duct obstructions resolve by 12 months with Crigler lacrimal sac massage and warm compresses. If watering persists past 12 to 13 months, lacrimal probing succeeds in 80 to 90 percent at first attempt under general anaesthesia.

faqs.question: Can long-term eye drops cause watering eyes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Long-term preservative-containing eye drops, especially glaucoma medications with benzalkonium chloride, inflame and scar the puncta and canaliculi. Preservative-free formulations can stabilize early stenosis; established stenosis may need punctal dilation or intubation.

faqs.question: Is watery eye linked to chemotherapy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Docetaxel and 5-fluorouracil are secreted in tears and can damage the canalicular epithelium. Up to 30 percent of patients on long-term docetaxel develop epiphora. Early use of artificial tears, punctal plugs, or silicone intubation can prevent progression to fixed stenosis.

faqs.question: Can watering eyes affect my vision?
faqs.answer: Watering eyes do not damage the optic nerve, but the constant tear film can cause intermittent blurring that clears with a hard blink. Severe untreated infection (orbital cellulitis) from neglected dacryocystitis can threaten vision, so acute swelling at the inner corner needs urgent care.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from DCR surgery?
faqs.answer: Most patients return to light activity within 1 week and to full activity within 2 to 4 weeks. Bruising and swelling at the medial canthus settle over 2 weeks. Silicone stents, if placed, are removed at 3 to 6 months. The final symptomatic result is judged at 3 to 6 months postoperatively.

faqs.question: Is endoscopic DCR better than external DCR?
faqs.answer: Both approaches have comparable long-term symptomatic success — around 90 percent in modern audits. Endoscopic DCR avoids a facial scar and is preferred when nasal pathology must be addressed. External DCR has the longest track record and is preferred in revision surgery.

faqs.question: Can I prevent epiphora?
faqs.answer: You can reduce risk by treating blepharitis and dry eye early, switching to preservative-free drops in long-term glaucoma therapy, controlling allergic rhinitis, protecting the face from mid-face trauma, and seeking review for any infant whose eye stays persistently wet.

faqs.question: How much does DCR surgery cost?
faqs.answer: Costs vary by country. In the UK, NHS DCR is free at the point of care; private rates are roughly 3,000 to 5,000 GBP. In the US, charges typically range from 4,000 to 12,000 USD by facility and insurance. In India, private centres charge 30,000 to 90,000 INR per side.

symptomKeywords: lump near inner corner of eye
metaDescription: Epiphora: overflow tearing from blocked tear ducts or surface irritation. Causes, diagnosis, DCR surgery, and recovery reviewed by our medical board team.

images.altText: Anatomical diagram of the lacrimal apparatus showing the lacrimal gland, puncta, canaliculi, lacrimal sac, and nasolacrimal duct draining into the inferior meatus of the nose
images.caption: Anatomy of the lacrimal drainage system — tears flow from the puncta through the canaliculi and sac to the nasolacrimal duct.

images.altText: Classic Gray's Anatomy plate showing the bony nasolacrimal canal and the relationship of the lacrimal sac to the medial canthal tendon
images.caption: Bony nasolacrimal canal and lacrimal sac — the anatomic basis of acquired NLDO.

images.altText: Clinical photograph showing acute dacryocystitis with a red, swollen lacrimal sac below the medial canthus — a serious complication of chronic epiphora
images.caption: Acute dacryocystitis — red, swollen tear sac arising from chronic nasolacrimal duct obstruction.
